text: The surgeon mobilizes the greater omentum to access the lesser sac and posterior gastric wall, preparing for a minimally invasive gastrointestinal bypass. A side-to-side mechanical gastrojejunal anastomosis is performed to bypass the stricture in the duodenal fourth portion caused by Crohn's disease.

The surgeon identifies the trait's ligament and explores the abdominal cavity and short bowel. The video reveals typical L-four involvement affecting the jejunum, characterized by creeping fat and congestion of the intestinal serosa.
The surgical team performs a duodenal jejunal anastomosis to treat the duodenal stenosis and obstructions, avoiding a gastric anastomosis to minimize bile reflux.

The surgeon performs a cohort maneuver to release the duodenum, followed by placing a reference duodenal jejunal suture to aid traction. A side-to-side duodenal jejunal mechanical anastomosis is executed, and the enterotomy is closed with a continuous running suture using long-term absorbable material.
